corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Motivated by these works, we propose new generic group-wise normalization for deep neural networks.

context_window.previous: In fact, a well-accepted computational model in neuroscience is to normalize across the cell responses [21, 52, 55, 5], “with various receptive-field centers (cov- ering the visual field) and with various spatiotemporal fre- quency tunings” (p183, [21]); this can happen not only in the primary visual cortex, but also “throughout the visual system” [5].
context_window.current: Motivated by these works, we propose new generic group-wise normalization for deep neural networks.

location.sentence: Implementation GN can be easily implemented by a few lines of code in PyTorch [42] and TensorFlow [1] where automatic differ- entiation is supported.

context_window.current: Implementation GN can be easily implemented by a few lines of code in PyTorch [42] and TensorFlow [1] where automatic differ- entiation is supported.
context_window.next: Figure 3 shows the code based on def GroupNorm(x, gamma, beta, G, eps=1e − 5): # x: input features with shape [N,C,H,W] # gamma, beta: scale and offset, with shape [1,C,1,1] # G: number of groups for GN N, C, H, W = x.shape x = tf.reshape(x, [N, G, C // G, H, W]) mean, var = tf.nn.moments(x, [2, 3, 4], keep dims=True) x = (x − mean) / tf.sqrt(var + eps) x = tf.reshape(x, [N, C, H, W]) return x ∗ gamma + beta Figure 3.

location.sentence: These features are often processed by group- wise normalization over each histogram or each orientation.

top_candidates.sentence: Next we compare GN and BN * on Mask R-CNN using a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) backbone , the currently state-of-the-art framework in COCO.
As a reference, with synchronous BN , a concurrent work achieves a from-scratch result of 34.5 box AP using R50 (Table ), and 36.3 using a specialized backbone.
The object detection and segmentation accuracy is statistically similar between these pre-trained models. layers to construct a pyramid, and appends randomly initialized layers as the head.
The 64-frame variant of GN has 74.5 / 91.7 accuracy, showing healthy gains over its BN counterpart and all BN variants.
GN helps the model benefit from temporal length, and the longer clip boosts the top-1 accuracy by 1.7% (top-5 1.1%) with the same batch size.
In this paper, we present Group Normalization (GN) as a simple alternative to BN.
GN's computation is independent of batch sizes, and its accuracy is stable in a wide range of batch sizes.

On ResNet-50 trained in ImageNet, GN has 10.6% lower error than its BN counterpart when using a batch size of 2; when using typical batch sizes, GN is comparably good with BN and outperforms other normalization variants.

GN can outperform its BNbased counterparts for object detection and segmentation in COCO, 1 and for video classification in Kinetics, showing that GN can effectively replace the powerful BN in a variety of tasks.
The usage of BN often requires these systems to compromise between the model design and batch sizes.
Analogously, we propose GN as a layer that divides channels into groups and normalizes the features within each group (Figure ).
With a regular batch size, GN is comparably good as BN (with a gap of ∼0.5%) and outperforms other normalization variants .
But the concept of "batch" is not always present, or it may change from time to time.
These methods do not suffer from the issues caused by the batch dimension, but they have not been able to approach BN's accuracy in many visual recognition tasks.
But BR is also batch-dependent, and when the batch size decreases its accuracy still degrades .

However, this method does not solve the problem of small batches; instead, it migrates the algorithm problem to engineering and hardware demands, using a number of GPUs proportional to BN's requirements.
The concept of groups as a dimension for model design has been more widely studied recently.
The work of ResNeXt investigates the trade-off between depth, width, and groups, and it suggests that a larger number of groups can improve accuracy under similar computational cost.
Next we compare GN and BN * on Mask R-CNN using a Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) backbone , the currently state-of-the-art framework in COCO.
BR has better accuracy than BN in the small-batch regime.
